_source: Curated in tools/plants-curated.json; distribution and photographs from the USDA NRCS PLANTS Database (public domain). Generated by tools/fetch_plants.mjs — do not hand-edit.

plants.description: Tall, 3–8 ft, hollow stems streaked or blotched purple. Umbrella-shaped clusters of small white flowers. Leaves twice or thrice divided, leaflet veins ending in the NOTCHES between teeth rather than at the tips.
plants.habitat: Wet ground — stream banks, ditches, marshes, wet meadows.
plants.season: Flowers early to midsummer.
plants.effect: The most violently toxic plant in North America. Cicutoxin causes seizures within 15–60 minutes and is frequently fatal. A piece of root the size of a walnut can kill an adult.
plants.tell: Cut the root lengthwise: it is CHAMBERED, with hollow cross-partitions, and oozes a yellow resin that smells of raw parsnip. No edible root in this family does that.

plants.description: 4–10 ft, smooth HAIRLESS stems with distinct purple blotches. Fern-like, finely divided leaves. Flat-topped umbrella clusters of tiny white flowers.
plants.habitat: Roadsides, ditches, waste ground, field edges. Widespread weed.
plants.season: Second-year plants flower late spring to midsummer.
plants.effect: Coniine causes ascending paralysis — it killed Socrates. Death is by respiratory failure while the mind stays clear. Toxic by ingestion; sap can also blister skin in sunlight.
plants.tell: Crush a leaf: a rank, musty, mouse-like stink. Wild carrot smells like carrot; this does not. Stems are hairless and purple-blotched — wild carrot's stem is hairy and unblotched.

plants.description: Grass-like basal leaves rising from a bulb, with a spike of small cream, greenish-white or yellowish six-petalled flowers. Bulb has a dark outer coat.
plants.habitat: Meadows, grassland, sagebrush, open woods — often the same damp spring meadows as edible camas.
plants.season: Flowers spring to early summer, when the bulbs are dug.
plants.effect: Zygacine causes vomiting, collapsing blood pressure and cardiac arrest. A few bulbs can kill an adult; it regularly kills livestock.
plants.tell: NO ONION SMELL. Every wild onion smells of onion when crushed — death camas never does. If a bulb does not smell of onion, it is not an onion. Flowers are cream or greenish, never blue.

plants.description: Stout unbranched stalk 3–7 ft with large, broad, heavily PLEATED oval leaves clasping the stem in a spiral. Dense panicles of star-shaped green or white flowers.
plants.habitat: Wet mountain meadows, seeps, stream sides, damp woods.
plants.season: Shoots emerge in spring — the dangerous stage, when they resemble edible shoots.
plants.effect: Steroidal alkaloids cause vomiting, dangerously slow heart rate and collapsing blood pressure. Poisoning from spring shoots mistaken for ramps or skunk cabbage is well documented.
plants.tell: Leaves are strongly pleated like a folded fan and clasp the stem alternately up a stout stalk. Ramps have smooth flat leaves from a bulb at ground level and smell strongly of onion; this has no onion smell.

plants.description: 2–6 ft, deeply palm-lobed leaves and unmistakable hooded blue-purple flowers, the top petal forming a helmet.
plants.habitat: Damp mountain meadows, stream banks, moist woods.
plants.season: Flowers midsummer to early autumn.
plants.effect: Aconitine is absorbed through skin as well as gut, causing numbness, arrhythmia and cardiac arrest. Handling the root with broken skin has poisoned people.
plants.tell: The hooded flower is diagnostic. Roots have been mistaken for wild horseradish — but no edible root grows under a blue hooded flower. Wear gloves.

plants.description: 1–3 ft, coarsely toothed compound leaves, a short cluster of white flowers, then a tight cluster of glossy white or red berries each on a thick stalk with a dark eye-spot.
plants.habitat: Shady moist woods, often the same ground as edible woodland berries.
plants.season: Berries ripen midsummer to autumn.
plants.effect: As few as six berries cause cardiac arrest in children; the berries are sweet, which is what makes them dangerous.
plants.tell: Berries sit in a stiff upright cluster on THICK stalks, each with a black dot like a pupil. No edible woodland berry looks like an eyeball on a red stem.

plants.description: First year a flat rosette of soft, grey-green, downy, veined leaves; second year a 2–5 ft spike of drooping tubular purple or white bells, spotted inside.
plants.habitat: Clearings, roadsides, logged ground, forest edges. Abundant in the Pacific Northwest.
plants.season: Rosette all year; flowers early to midsummer.
plants.effect: Cardiac glycosides. Causes nausea, visual disturbance and fatal arrhythmia. The first-year rosette — before any flower exists to warn you — is the dangerous stage.
plants.tell: Leaves are alternate and softly downy with a strong net of veins, and there is no smell. Comfrey leaves are bristly-hairy; mullein's are thick, pale and felted. If unsure, do not.

plants.description: Poison ivy and oak: three leaflets, the middle one on a longer stalk, glossy and often red-tinged in spring and autumn. Poison sumac: 7–13 leaflets on a red stalk, in swamps. White or cream berries.
plants.habitat: Forest edges, trails, riverbanks, fencelines — poison sumac only in wet ground.
plants.season: All year. Bare winter stems still carry the oil.
plants.effect: Urushiol causes severe blistering dermatitis, usually 12–72 hours later. Burning it and inhaling the smoke can cause life-threatening airway swelling. Do not use it as firewood.
plants.tell: Leaves of three, let it be — with the middle leaflet on a noticeably longer stalk. Berries are WHITE; the edible staghorn sumac carries an upright red cone of fuzzy berries.

plants.description: Large 4–10 ft plant with a smooth, striking magenta-red stem, big oval leaves, and drooping clusters of dark purple berries.
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, field edges, clearings. Eastern and central US.
plants.season: Shoots in spring, berries late summer to autumn.
plants.effect: Root, mature stem and seeds contain phytolaccatoxin — severe gastroenteritis, sometimes fatal in children. Traditionally eaten as 'poke sallet' only as young shoots, boiled in several changes of water; the practice injures people every year.
plants.tell: The magenta stem and drooping purple berry clusters are distinctive. Any red in the stem means the shoot is too old to have been used at all.

plants.description: 2–5 ft, coarse jagged-toothed leaves with a rank smell, large white or violet trumpet flowers, and an egg-sized SPINY seed capsule.
plants.habitat: Waste ground, overgrazed pasture, barnyards, disturbed soil.
plants.season: Flowers summer, seed capsules autumn.
plants.effect: Tropane alkaloids cause delirium, hyperthermia, and death. Dose varies enormously plant to plant, which is why deliberate use kills people too.
plants.tell: The spiny thornapple capsule is unmistakable, and the whole plant stinks when bruised.

plants.description: Sprawling 1–3 ft herb, oval leaves, small white star flowers with yellow centres, and clusters of small green-to-black berries.
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, gardens, field edges, clearings.
plants.season: Berries late summer to autumn.
plants.effect: Solanine, concentrated in unripe green berries and foliage — gastroenteritis, and neurological effects at higher dose. Some species are eaten when fully ripe in some cultures, which is exactly the ambiguity that gets people hurt.
plants.tell: Small star-shaped white flowers with a yellow cone, and berries in loose clusters off the stem. Treat every green berry as poisonous.

plants.description: Evergreen shrub or tree, flat dark-green needles in two ranks, and a soft red berry-like cup — open at the end — holding a single dark seed.
plants.habitat: Shady forest understory, and very widely planted around buildings.
plants.season: Red arils late summer to autumn.
plants.effect: Taxine alkaloids cause sudden cardiac arrest, often with no useful warning. Needles, bark and the SEED are lethal; there is no reliable antidote.
plants.tell: The red cup is open at the tip with the seed visible inside — no true berry looks like that. The flesh alone is famously non-toxic, but the seed inside it is deadly, so treat the whole thing as poison.

plants.description: Reddish-brown to dark cap that is wrinkled, lobed and BRAIN-like — folded rather than pitted. Stem is stuffed with cottony tissue or irregularly chambered.
plants.habitat: Conifer woods and sandy disturbed ground, in spring alongside true morels.
plants.season: Spring, the same weeks as morels.
plants.effect: Gyromitrin metabolises to monomethylhydrazine — rocket fuel. Causes liver failure and death; the toxin is volatile, so people have been poisoned by the steam while cooking it.
plants.tell: Cut it top to bottom. A true morel is COMPLETELY HOLLOW from cap tip to stem base, and its cap is pitted like a honeycomb and attached at the bottom. A false morel is solid or chambered inside and the cap hangs free like a skirt.

plants.description: White to greenish cap, WHITE gills, a ring on the stem, and — the critical feature — a cup-like sac (volva) around the base, usually buried.
plants.habitat: Woodland, often near oak, and on lawns and park edges.
plants.season: Late summer and autumn, and spring in some regions.
plants.effect: Amatoxins destroy the liver. Symptoms are delayed 6–24 hours, appear to improve, then organ failure follows. Responsible for most fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. Half a cap can kill.
plants.tell: DIG UP THE WHOLE BASE — never cut at ground level. A sac-like cup around the base plus white gills means walk away. Field mushrooms have pink then chocolate-brown gills and no cup; puffballs must be pure white and featureless when sliced in half, with no outline of a mushroom inside.

plants.description: 2–4 ft, finely divided fern-like leaves, and a flat lacy umbrella of tiny white flowers usually with a single dark purple floret at the centre. The cluster curls into a bird's-nest shape as it seeds.
plants.habitat: Roadsides, fields, meadows, disturbed ground.
plants.season: First-year roots autumn to spring; flowers midsummer.
plants.edibleParts: First-year taproot, cooked. Woody and inedible in the second year.
plants.tell: HAIRY stems — 'the Queen has hairy legs' — and it smells strongly of carrot when crushed. Poison hemlock is hairless with purple blotches and smells musty; water hemlock has a chambered root. If it does not smell of carrot, it is not carrot.

plants.description: Grass-like or strap leaves from an underground bulb, with a rounded umbel of small pink, white or purple flowers on a leafless stalk.
plants.habitat: Meadows, open woods, grassland, damp ground.
plants.season: Spring and early summer.
plants.tell: It MUST smell of onion or garlic when crushed — that smell is the whole test, and it is decisive. Death camas grows in the same meadows, looks similar, and has no smell at all.

plants.description: Grass-like basal leaves and a tall spike of showy six-petalled BLUE to violet flowers, from a deep bulb.
plants.habitat: Wet spring meadows, prairies, oak openings — a Pacific Northwest staple food.
plants.season: Flowers late spring; bulbs dug after flowering.
plants.edibleParts: Bulb, slow-cooked for many hours — the classic first-foods pit-roast.
plants.tell: Only dig camas WHILE IT IS IN FLOWER, and only blue-flowered plants. Death camas grows in the same meadows with cream or greenish flowers, and once the flowers are gone the two bulbs cannot be told apart.

plants.description: Two or three smooth, flat, broad leaves rising directly from a white bulb at ground level, often with a purple-tinged leaf stalk.
plants.habitat: Rich moist deciduous woods, eastern North America.
plants.season: Early spring, before the tree canopy closes.
plants.edibleParts: Leaves and bulb. Slow to recover from harvest — take leaves, not bulbs.
plants.tell: Strong onion-garlic smell, and smooth FLAT leaves from a bulb at ground level. False hellebore has pleated leaves clasping a stout upright stalk and no smell; lily-of-the-valley also has no smell and is poisonous.

plants.description: Cap deeply PITTED like a honeycomb, ridges usually paler than the pits, and the cap attached to the stem at its base.
plants.habitat: Woodland, old burns, orchards, disturbed ground, often near ash, elm, apple or cottonwood.
plants.season: Spring.
plants.edibleParts: Whole fungus, always thoroughly cooked. Raw morels are toxic even when correctly identified.
plants.tell: Slice it top to bottom: a true morel is ENTIRELY HOLLOW from cap tip to stem base, one continuous cavity. False morels are solid, cottony or chambered inside, and their brain-like cap hangs free rather than joining at the base.

plants.description: Low to medium shrubs with small oval alternate leaves and round berries, blue-black to red, carrying a five-pointed crown at the blossom end.
plants.habitat: Acid soils — conifer forest, mountain slopes, bogs, burns and clearings.
plants.season: Berries midsummer to early autumn.
plants.tell: The five-pointed star crown at the end of the berry, and berries growing singly or in small leafy clusters on a woody shrub. Baneberry sits in a stiff cluster on thick stalks with a dark eye; nightshade is a sprawling soft herb with star-shaped flowers.

plants.description: Large shrub or small tree with long compound leaves of 11–31 toothed leaflets, and a dense UPRIGHT cone of fuzzy deep-red berries at the branch tips.
plants.habitat: Roadsides, old fields, forest edges, dry slopes.
plants.season: Red cones from late summer, persisting into winter.
plants.edibleParts: The red berry cluster, steeped in cold water for a tart lemonade. Never boiled — that draws out bitter tannins.
plants.tell: Berries are RED, fuzzy, and stand upright in a tight cone. Poison sumac has loose, drooping WHITE berries and grows only in standing water or bog.

plants.description: Tall strap-like leaves from a rhizome in shallow water, topped by the unmistakable brown cigar-shaped seed head.
plants.habitat: Marshes, pond and ditch margins, slow water.
plants.season: Shoots spring, pollen early summer, rhizomes autumn and winter.
plants.edibleParts: Young inner shoots, green flower spikes, pollen, and rhizome starch.
plants.tell: Before the brown seed head forms, cattail shoots resemble iris and false hellebore. Cattail leaves are flat and round-tipped and come from a spongy shoot in water; iris leaves are flattened in a fan and its rhizome is poisonous. Only gather where the old brown seed heads are visible.

plants.description: Classic mushroom shape, white to brown cap, a ring on the stem, and gills that start PINK and darken to chocolate brown then black.
plants.habitat: Pasture, lawns, meadows, woodland edges.
plants.season: Late summer and autumn.
plants.tell: Gills must be pink or brown, never white, and there must be NO cup-like sac at the base. Dig the whole base out to check — a destroying angel has white gills and a buried volva, and it kills.

plants.description: 2–6 ft in shallow water, with leaves ONCE divided into a single row of narrow, sharply saw-toothed leaflets, and flat white umbrella flower clusters.
plants.habitat: Marshes, slow streams, pond edges — the same water as water hemlock.
plants.season: Roots autumn and spring; flowers midsummer.
plants.edibleParts: Root, cooked. Widely eaten historically, and widely regretted by people who got the identification wrong.
plants.tell: Leaves are divided ONCE, into a single row of leaflets. Water hemlock's leaves are divided two or three times, and its root is chambered. Both grow in the same ditch. If you cannot see the root, do not take the plant.

plants.description: 2–5 ft with a grooved stem and flat clusters of YELLOW flowers — the only common wild carrot-family plant with yellow umbels.
plants.habitat: Roadsides, old fields, disturbed ground.
plants.season: Flowers midsummer.
plants.effect: Sap contains furanocoumarins: on skin in sunlight it causes phytophotodermatitis — severe blistering burns that scar for months. The root is edible, which is exactly why people handle the plant and get burned.
plants.tell: Yellow flowers, not white. Handle only with covered arms, and never crush the foliage on bare skin in sunlight.

plants.description: 4–8 ft, blue-green thread-fine feathery foliage, yellow umbrella flower clusters, from a stout bulbous base.
plants.habitat: Roadsides, coastal scrub, waste ground. Abundant in California and the West.
plants.season: Foliage spring to autumn; seeds late summer.
plants.edibleParts: Foliage, seeds, and the swollen stem base.
plants.tell: It smells overwhelmingly of ANISE or liquorice when crushed, and its flowers are yellow. Poison hemlock is thicker-leaved, purple-blotched, and smells rank and musty. The smell decides it.

plants.description: Huge waxy bright-green oval leaves up to 5 ft, from a boggy rosette, with a vivid yellow hood around a green flower spike in early spring.
plants.habitat: Swamps, seeps, wet woodland — Pacific Northwest.
plants.season: Flowers very early spring; leaves through summer.
plants.effect: Calcium oxalate raphides — needle crystals that cause intense burning and swelling of mouth and throat. Not usually fatal, but the swelling can close an airway.
plants.tell: Skunky smell and a bright yellow hood in early spring. Its leaves are smooth and rise from the ground; false hellebore's are pleated and clasp a tall stalk.

images.credit: Unknown authorUnknown author or not provided (Public domain) · Wikimedia Commons

plants.description: 1–6 ft with deeply palm-lobed leaves and spikes of blue-purple flowers, each with a backward-pointing SPUR.
plants.habitat: Meadows, open woods, sagebrush slopes.
plants.season: Flowers late spring to summer.
plants.effect: Diterpene alkaloids cause muscular paralysis and respiratory failure. The leading killer of cattle on western ranges.
plants.tell: The flower has a straight spur pointing backwards; monkshood's has a hood instead. Both are poisonous, so the distinction is academic in the field — avoid either.

plants.description: 2–4 ft with large, coarse, BRISTLY-hairy lance-shaped leaves running down the stem, and drooping bells of purple, pink or cream.
plants.habitat: Damp ground, riverbanks, old gardens gone wild.
plants.season: Leaves spring to autumn; flowers early summer.
plants.effect: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids cause cumulative, irreversible liver damage. Long used as a herbal poultice and tea; the tea is now understood to be harmful.
plants.tell: Rough bristly hairs that catch on the skin, and leaf bases that run down the stem as wings. Foxglove is softly downy with a fine net of veins and no wings.

plants.description: First year a large rosette of thick, pale, FELTED leaves like flannel; second year a single stout spike 3–7 ft with small yellow flowers.
plants.habitat: Dry disturbed ground, roadsides, burns, gravel.
plants.season: Rosette all year; flower spike midsummer.
plants.edibleParts: Leaves and flowers as a strained tea, traditionally for coughs. The fine hairs must be strained out — they irritate the throat.
plants.tell: Leaves are thick, pale grey-green and densely woolly — unmistakably like felt. Foxglove's rosette is thinner, darker, softly downy rather than woolly, and deadly.

plants.description: A featureless ball, white to buff, with no cap, no gills and no stem, sitting on the ground or on wood.
plants.habitat: Pasture, lawns, woodland, old fields.
plants.season: Late summer and autumn.
plants.edibleParts: Flesh, cooked, only while it is PURE WHITE throughout.
plants.tell: Slice every one top to bottom before cooking. It must be uniformly white and featureless like a marshmallow. If you can see the faint outline of a mushroom — a cap, gills, a stem — inside, it is an unopened Amanita button and it will kill you. Any yellow or brown inside means it is too old.

plants.description: Two or three broad smooth leaves from a creeping rhizome, with a one-sided spray of small nodding white bells, then red-orange berries.
plants.habitat: Shady woods, old gardens and their escapes.
plants.season: Flowers spring, berries late summer.
plants.effect: Cardiac glycosides throughout the plant, including the water it has stood in. Causes vomiting and fatal arrhythmia; children have been poisoned by the berries.
plants.tell: NO onion smell — that is the whole test against ramps, whose leaves it closely resembles in spring. Its leaves come from a creeping rhizome rather than a distinct white bulb.

plants.description: Flat sword-shaped leaves overlapping in a FAN at the base, and showy three-part blue, purple or yellow flowers.
plants.habitat: Marshes, wet meadows, pond margins — with the cattails.
plants.season: Flowers late spring to early summer.
plants.effect: Irisin in the rhizome causes violent vomiting and diarrhoea; sap irritates skin. Poisonings happen when the rhizome is dug for food in spring.
plants.tell: Leaves are flattened and overlap in a single plane like a hand of cards; cattail leaves are rounder, thicker and come from a spongy shoot. Before the brown cattail head appears, treat every unidentified marsh shoot as iris.
